text: We present a robotic-assisted single-port pyeloplasty in an eighty-year-old female. She initially presented the emergency room with right flank pain, nausea, vomiting, and a CT scan was obtained which revealed right hydronephrosis. There was no obvious ureteral stone or ureteral mass causing the obstruction and the pattern was in a typical UPJ type obstruction. Shenowen MAC-III renal scan, which revealed a delayed T-one-half of one hundred twenty minutes on the right kidney versus six point five minutes on the left kidney and a reduced function on the right kidney of thirty percent.

We decided to proceed with a single port robotic right pyeloplasty. I placed the single port at the umbilicus and I had the patient in a modified flank position. For this case I did end up using a plus one with an eight millimeter air seal. I did not think I needed a liver retractor port for this procedure and I was able to do it without one. We start the case by getting into the retroperitoneal space. For the orientation of the arms I put the camera at twelve o'clock for this procedure and the scissors at six o'clock and for the three o'clock and nine o'clock positions I put two fenestrated bipolars. The reason I don't use a Cartier is because the bipolar is pretty sufficient and in case I want to use the right hand as a bipolar I could just plug it in. I find that this is a little bit more versatile.

The surgeon identifies the junction between the ureter and renal pelvis, noting the presence of a crossing vessel, which is the main artery and vein. The renal pelvis is mobilized superior to these vessels, with careful manipulation in both anterior and posterior positions.

The surgeon performs a diamond-shaped excision of the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) after fully mobilizing the renal pelvis. The medial surface of the renal pelvis is carried to its superior aspect, followed by excision of tissue both anteriorly and posteriorly as part of the reduction pyeloplasty.

The surgeon begins closing the renal pelvis using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, creating self-retraction by hooking it to the perineum. The ureter is spatulated on the lateral aspect, and the UPJ holder area is prepared for excision. The anastomosis is initiated on the renal pelvis side using a fibroviral suture, focusing on the posterior aspect due to its complexity.

The surgeon places a stent in an antegrade fashion after partially completing the anastomosis on each side. The UPJ is excised and sent for pathology. Methylene blue is used to backfill the bladder, with a hint of blue visible, confirming the stent's placement. The danune hook is removed to complete the renal pelvis closure, and the area is retroperitonealized.
